phases.phase1Goal: Define the company's long-term vision, mission, and core values that drive all initiatives.
phases.phase2Goal: Understand the macro market forces, industry trends, and competitive dynamics affecting the entire company portfolio.
phases.phase3Goal: Define resource allocation across device lines and strategic initiatives to achieve company goals.
phases.phase4Goal: Establish company-wide policies for decision-making, risk management, and integrated ESG principles.
phases.phase5Goal: Define and track critical metrics measuring company progress against strategic goals.
phases.phase6Goal: Ensure the strategic vision is clearly communicated and embedded throughout the organization.

activities.activity1Desc: Define the company's ultimate aspiration and purpose in healthcare.
activities.activity1Questions: What is our ultimate aspiration? What do we aim to achieve for healthcare? What core values guide our decisions? How do we want to impact patients, clinicians, and the healthcare system?
activities.activity2Desc: Establish the key strategic areas that will achieve the company vision.
activities.activity2Questions: What are the key strategic areas (e.g., Market Expansion, Innovation, Operational Excellence, Sustainability Leadership)? What are the high-level goals for each pillar? How do these pillars align with market trends?
activities.activity3Desc: Define the ethical principles and values that guide all company decisions.
activities.activity3Questions: What are our non-negotiable values? How do we balance innovation with patient safety? What is our commitment to ethical practices, social responsibility, and environmental stewardship?
activities.activity4Title: Define Success Metrics for Strategic Vision
activities.activity4Desc: Establish how the company will measure success against its vision.
activities.activity4Questions: What does success look like in 3-5 years? What key milestones indicate we're on track? How will we know if our strategic pillars are working?
activities.activity5Desc: Analyze overarching industry trends and regulatory shifts impacting the business.
activities.activity5Questions: What are the overarching industry trends (e.g., digital health, AI, telehealth)? What global regulatory shifts (e.g., EU Green Deal, new FDA guidances) impact our entire business? What demographic changes affect our markets?
activities.activity6Desc: Identify primary enterprise competitors and differentiation strategy.
activities.activity6Questions: Who are our primary enterprise competitors? What are their strengths, weaknesses, and overall market share? How do we differentiate at the company level? What are our unique capabilities?
activities.activity7Desc: Conduct comprehensive strengths, weaknesses, opportunities, and threats analysis.
activities.activity7Questions: What are our inherent strengths and weaknesses as an organization? What external opportunities and threats exist for the entire business? How can we leverage our strengths to capture opportunities? What weaknesses must we address urgently?
activities.activity8Desc: Map emerging technologies and innovation opportunities relevant to the portfolio.
activities.activity8Questions: What emerging technologies could disrupt our markets? What innovation opportunities align with our strategic pillars? Should we invest in R&D, partnerships, or acquisitions?
activities.activity9Desc: Determine the desired balance for the device portfolio.
activities.activity9Questions: What is the desired balance for our portfolio (e.g., growth vs. mature devices, high-risk/high-reward vs. stable)? How will we manage device lifecycle transitions? What devices should we sunset or divest?
activities.activity10Desc: Define how resources will be allocated across strategic initiatives.
activities.activity10Questions: What are our key resource constraints (e.g., R&D, sales, regulatory personnel)? How will we allocate these across strategic pillars and device categories? What is our decision framework for resource allocation?
activities.activity11Desc: Establish the overall investment strategy and capital distribution.
activities.activity11Questions: What is our overall investment strategy (e.g., aggressive growth, sustainable profit)? How will capital be distributed across initiatives? What is our funding roadmap?
activities.activity12Desc: Plan for managing devices across their lifecycle stages.
activities.activity12Questions: How will we transition devices from development to maturity? What are our strategies for extending device lifecycles? When should we discontinue devices?
activities.activity13Desc: Determine technology platforms and architecture decisions.
activities.activity13Questions: What core technology platforms will we build on? Should we develop proprietary platforms or leverage existing ones? How do platform decisions affect scalability and innovation?
activities.activity14Desc: Define how strategic decisions are made, reviewed, and approved.
activities.activity14Questions: How are strategic decisions made, reviewed, and approved? What are our ethical conduct guidelines? Who has authority for different types of decisions? What is our board governance structure?
activities.activity15Desc: Define the company's overall risk tolerance and mitigation strategies.
activities.activity15Questions: What is the company's overall tolerance for financial, operational, market, and regulatory risks? What are our top 3 enterprise-level risks and their mitigation strategies? How do we balance risk and innovation?
activities.activity16Desc: Establish commitments to environmental sustainability, social responsibility, and ethical governance.
activities.activity16Questions: What are our commitments to environmental sustainability, social responsibility, and ethical governance? Which key standards (e.g., ISO 14001, UN SDGs) will we adhere to? What are our targets for carbon reduction, sustainable sourcing, and waste management?
activities.activity17Desc: Define regulatory compliance and ethical business practices.
activities.activity17Questions: What regulatory standards must we comply with globally? What are our ethical guidelines for business conduct? How do we ensure compliance across all operations?
activities.activity18Desc: Plan for managing relationships with key stakeholders.
activities.activity18Questions: Who are our key stakeholders (investors, customers, regulators, employees, communities)? How will we engage and communicate with each? What are their expectations and how do we meet them?
activities.activity23Desc: Identify the most critical metrics for measuring strategic success.
activities.activity23Questions: What are the 5-10 most critical KPIs for the company (e.g., Revenue Growth, Market Share, Regulatory Approval Rate, Carbon Footprint Reduction)? How will these be measured? Who is responsible for each KPI?
activities.activity24Desc: Define key financial metrics and targets.
activities.activity24Questions: What are our revenue, profitability, and cash flow targets? What financial ratios are most important? How do we measure return on investment?
activities.activity25Desc: Establish metrics for market share and growth.
activities.activity25Questions: How do we measure market share in our segments? What are our growth rate targets? How do we track customer acquisition and retention?
activities.activity26Desc: Define metrics for innovation and R&D effectiveness.
activities.activity26Questions: What percentage of revenue comes from new devices? How do we measure R&D productivity? What are our patent and IP metrics?
activities.activity27Desc: Establish metrics for environmental, social, and governance performance.
activities.activity27Questions: How do we measure our carbon footprint? What are our diversity and inclusion metrics? How do we track sustainability initiatives?
activities.activity28Desc: Define how and when KPIs will be reviewed.
activities.activity28Questions: How frequently will we review the Company Venture Blueprint (e.g., quarterly, annually)? Who is responsible for monitoring? What triggers a strategic review?
activities.activity29Desc: Plan how to communicate the strategic blueprint across the organization.
activities.activity29Questions: How will we communicate the Company Venture Blueprint to all employees, stakeholders, and the board? What are our key messages? What channels will we use?
activities.activity30Desc: Foster a culture that supports both innovation and regulatory compliance.
activities.activity30Questions: How do we foster a culture that supports both rapid innovation and unwavering compliance? What behaviors do we reward? How do we handle failure and learning?
activities.activity31Questions: How will we manage resistance to change? What training and support will employees need? How do we ensure smooth transitions?
activities.activity32Desc: Plan for ongoing stakeholder communication and engagement.
activities.activity32Questions: How do we keep stakeholders informed and engaged? What feedback mechanisms will we establish? How do we build trust and transparency?
activities.activity33Desc: Define how the company positions itself in the market.
activities.activity33Questions: What is our unique value proposition? How do we want to be perceived by customers, partners, and competitors? What is our brand promise?

1.title: Identify Clinical or User Need
1.description: Define the specific, unmet clinical need and who is most affected by this problem.

2.title: Competitor Analysis
2.description: Identify current solutions, competitors, and what makes your approach different.

3.title: Market Sizing
3.description: Define the total addressable market (TAM), serviceable market (SAM), and target market (SOM).

4.title: Define Core Solution Concept
4.description: Create a clear concept brief describing your proposed solution and core technology.

5.title: Profile User
5.description: Define target patient population, intended operators, environment, duration, and what triggers device use.

6.title: Articulate Value Proposition
6.description: Define the measurable benefit of your solution with quantified improvements.

7.title: Regulatory & Compliance Assessment
7.description: Determine likely device classification and major standards that will apply.

8.title: Risk Assessment
8.description: Conduct preliminary hazard analysis per ISO 14971.

9.title: High-Level Project & Resource Plan
9.description: Outline major phases, estimate budget, team size, and create a high-level timeline.

10.title: System Architecture
10.description: Create high-level architecture including hardware, software, and materials.

11.title: Detailed Design & Prototyping
11.description: Create specifications, schematics, CAD drawings, and build prototypes.

12.title: Formal Risk Management (ISO 14971)
12.description: Conduct detailed risk analysis (FMEA) and implement control measures.

13.title: Design Verification
13.description: Execute tests to prove the device meets all system requirements.

14.title: Design Validation & Usability
14.description: Test with end-users in simulated or real clinical environments.

15.title: Manufacturing Process Validation
15.description: Validate manufacturing line can consistently produce to specification.

16.title: Compile Regulatory Submission
16.description: Gather all documentation into required regulatory format.

17.title: Go-to-Market Strategy
17.description: Finalize pricing, sales channels, marketing materials, and customer support plan.

18.title: Manufacturing & Supply Chain
18.description: Finalize supplier selection, production strategy, and supply chain logistics.

19.title: Submit for Approval
19.description: Formally submit to regulatory authorities and respond to questions.

20.title: Commercial Launch
20.description: Execute go-to-market plan and train sales force.

21.title: Post-Market Surveillance
21.description: Monitor device in field, handle complaints, track adverse events.

22.title: Analyze Performance
22.description: Analyze sales data, complaint trends, and PMS data.

23.title: Plan Next Version
23.description: Prioritize features for next device version based on collected data.

24.title: Maintain Compliance
24.description: Handle changes through formal change control process.

5b.title: Profile Economic Buyer
5b.description: Define market-specific economic buyer characteristics and budget ownership.

metrics.rnpvTooltip: Calculation: rNPV = NPV × (1 - Risk%). NPV = Σ (Revenue - Costs - Cannibalization) / (1 + Discount Rate)^year. Sum of risk-adjusted NPV across all markets, discounted to present value.
metrics.roiTooltip: Calculation: ROI = (rNPV / Total Investment) × 100. Average return on investment across all markets, showing profitability as a percentage of total costs.
metrics.riskTooltip: Calculation: Risk Factor = (Technical Risk + Regulatory Risk + Commercial Risk + Competitive Risk) / 4. Average risk percentage across four categories, weighted equally. Higher values indicate greater project risk.
metrics.investmentTooltip: Calculation: Total Investment = Development + Clinical Trials + Regulatory + Manufacturing + Marketing + Operations. Sum of all upfront and operational costs required across all markets during the first 5 years.
metrics.breakevenTooltip: Calculation: Breakeven Month = First month where Cumulative Cash Flow ≥ 0. Cumulative Cash Flow = Σ (Revenue - Costs - Development - Cannibalization). Time until all investments are recovered through positive cash flows.

statement.intendedUseTooltip: State the general, high-level medical purpose of the device. This should be a concise summary of why the device exists and what it is meant to achieve in a clinical or user context.
statement.intendedUsePlaceholder: Example: To provide an accessible method for individuals to monitor key cardiovascular health indicators and identify potential irregularities for further medical consultation.
statement.intendedFunctionLabel: Intended Function / Indications (The What and for What)
statement.intendedFunctionTooltip: Be specific. Describe what the device does (its function) and the specific disease, condition, or patient parameter it is intended to diagnose, treat, monitor, or screen for (its indication).
statement.intendedFunctionPlaceholder: Example: The device functions by capturing a single-lead electrocardiogram (ECG) and analyzing the rhythm for the presence of atrial fibrillation (AFib).
statement.modeOfActionTooltip: Explain the technical or physiological mechanism by which the device achieves its intended function. This is often required for novel technologies.
statement.modeOfActionPlaceholder: Example: The device uses photoplethysmography (PPG) to detect blood volume changes in peripheral circulation.
statement.valuePropositionTooltip: Describe the unique value your device provides to patients, clinicians, and payers. What makes it better than existing alternatives? Focus on clinical outcomes, cost savings, or workflow improvements.
statement.valuePropositionPlaceholder: Example: Our device reduces diagnosis time by 50% while improving accuracy by 30%, enabling earlier intervention and reducing overall healthcare costs by $2,000 per patient.

context.patientPopulationTooltip: Define the specific patient population for whom the device is intended.
context.intendedUserTooltip: Specify who will operate the device.
context.durationOfUseTooltip: Specify the MDR-compliant duration of continuous use: Transient (<60 min), Short-Term (60 min - 30 days), or Long-Term (>30 days). This is critical for device classification under EU MDR Annex VIII.
context.environmentTooltip: Describe where the device will be used.
context.triggerTooltip: What clinical event, condition, or situation initiates the use of this device? This is important for usability engineering and understanding the context of use.

safety.contraindicationsTooltip: List conditions or circumstances where the device should NOT be used.
safety.warningsTooltip: List important warnings or precautions users should be aware of.
safety.clinicalBenefitsTooltip: List the clinical benefits or advantages of using this device.

additional.userInstructionsTooltip: Provide clear instructions for users on how to use, charge, and maintain the device.
additional.howToUseDescription: Describe how to use the device...
additional.howToUsePlaceholder: Provide step-by-step instructions on how to properly use the device. Include any important setup steps, operational guidance, and best practices.
additional.chargingPlaceholder: Explain how to charge the device, including charging time, indicators, and any precautions. If the device doesn't require charging, note that here.
additional.maintenancePlaceholder: Describe how to clean, store, and maintain the device. Include recommended cleaning methods, storage conditions, and maintenance schedule.

classification.medicalDeviceTooltip: Any instrument, apparatus, appliance, software, implant, reagent, material or other article intended by the manufacturer to be used for medical purposes.
classification.ivdTooltip: Device intended to perform in vitro examination of specimens derived from the human body, primarily for diagnosis, prevention, monitoring of disease, or determining compatibility for blood transfusion.
classification.systemProcedurePackTooltip: Combination of products packaged together and placed on the market with the purpose of being used for a specific medical purpose.
classification.nonInvasiveTooltip: Device that does not penetrate inside the body, either through a body orifice or through the surface of the body.
classification.invasiveTooltip: Device that penetrates inside the body, either through a body orifice or through the surface of the body.
classification.surgicallyInvasiveTooltip: Device that penetrates inside the body through the surface of the body, by way of surgery.
classification.implantableTooltip: Device intended to be totally or partially introduced into the human body or a natural orifice by surgical intervention and intended to remain after the procedure.
classification.anatomicalLocationPlaceholder: Select anatomical location of device contact
classification.anatomicalLocationHelper: Critical for device classification - specify where the device contacts the body
classification.surfaceAreaHelper: Required for ISO 10993-17 toxicological risk assessment
classification.activeDeviceTooltip: Device depends on a source of energy (like electricity) to function. This does not include energy generated by the human body or gravity.

technical.trlTooltip: MedTech TRL scale (3-8). Levels 1-2 cover basic research before product development begins. TRL 3+ applies to active device development projects.
technical.isReusableTooltip: Device intended for multiple uses on different patients
technical.incorporatesMedicinalSubstanceTooltip: Contains medicinal substance as integral part
technical.containsHumanAnimalMaterialTooltip: Manufactured using tissues of human or animal origin
technical.isCustomMadeTooltip: Specially made according to written prescription
technical.isAccessoryToMedicalDeviceTooltip: Intended to support, complement or augment a medical device
technical.nonSterileDeviceTooltip: Device does not require sterility and is not intended to be sterile.
technical.deliveredSterileTooltip: Device is provided in a sterile state and ready to use without sterilization.
technical.canBeSterilizedTooltip: Device can be sterilized or re-sterilized by the user before use.

overview.description: Client Compass is your central hub for managing multiple clients and companies. It provides a visual overview of all companies you have access to, with status indicators and quick navigation to company-specific dashboards.
overview.whoUsesIt: All team members who work across multiple companies or clients use Client Compass to quickly navigate between different client projects and monitor their overall status.

keyBenefits.0: Visual overview of all your companies at a glance
keyBenefits.1: Quick status assessment with color-coded indicators
keyBenefits.3: Role-based access control for team collaboration
keyBenefits.4: Search and filter capabilities for large portfolios

0.title: Understanding Client Compass
0.content:
Client Compass displays all companies you have access to in a visual card grid. Each card represents a company and shows:

- Company name and logo
- Status indicator (On Track, At Risk, Needs Attention)
- Number of products
- Country/market information
- Your role in the company

The status indicators use a traffic light system:
- Green: On Track - everything is progressing well
- Yellow: At Risk - requires attention or monitoring
- Red: Needs Attention - immediate action required

Click on any company card to navigate to that company's Mission Control dashboard.

tips.0: Bookmark Client Compass as your daily starting point
tips.1: Review status indicators each morning to prioritize your work
tips.2: Use search to quickly find specific companies in large portfolios

1.title: Navigating Between Companies
1.content:
Use Client Compass to efficiently switch between different client projects:

1. From any page, click the company selector in the navigation
2. You'll see the Client Compass view with all your companies
3. Use the search bar to filter by company name
4. Click on a company card to switch to that company's workspace

When you select a company, the entire interface switches to that company's context, showing only relevant data, projects, and documents.

tips.0: The active company is shown in the top navigation bar
tips.1: Your recent companies appear at the top for quick access
tips.2: You can switch companies without losing your work in progress

2.title: Understanding Status Indicators
2.content:
Client Compass uses intelligent status indicators to help you prioritize:

On Track (Green):
- All deliverables are on schedule
- No overdue tasks or critical issues
- Compliance requirements are up to date

At Risk (Yellow):
- Some tasks are approaching deadlines
- Minor compliance gaps identified
- Requires monitoring and attention

Needs Attention (Red):
- Overdue deliverables or tasks
- Critical compliance issues
- Immediate action required

Status is calculated automatically based on:
- Task and milestone deadlines
- Document approval status
- Compliance gap analysis results
- Risk assessment findings

tips.0: Red status companies should be your first priority
tips.1: Set up notifications for status changes
tips.2: Review yellow status companies weekly to prevent escalation

3.title: Search and Filtering
3.content:
For users managing many companies, Client Compass provides powerful search and filtering:

Search by Company Name:
- Type in the search box to filter companies in real-time
- Search works on company name and aliases

Filter by Status:
- Show only companies needing attention
- Focus on at-risk companies
- View all companies on track

Filter by Country/Market:
- Group companies by regulatory region
- Focus on specific markets

Sort Options:
- Alphabetically by company name
- By status priority (red, yellow, green)
- By last accessed date

tips.0: Save common filter combinations for quick access
tips.1: Use status filtering for daily priority reviews
tips.2: Combine filters to create focused work lists

4.title: Understanding Your Roles
4.content:
Each company card displays your role, which determines your permissions and responsibilities:

Common Roles:
- Regulatory Manager: Full access to regulatory submissions and compliance
- Quality Manager: QMS and document control focus
- Project Manager: Project planning and timeline management
- Clinical Lead: Clinical trial oversight
- Team Member: View and contribute to assigned tasks

Your role affects:
- Which modules and features you can access
- What data you can view and modify
- Notification settings and alerts
- Approval workflows you participate in

Multiple roles are possible - you may have different roles in different companies.

tips.0: Check your role on each company card
tips.1: Contact your admin if you need additional permissions
tips.2: Roles are company-specific, not global

overview.description: Mission Control is your central dashboard for managing a single company or viewing aggregated data across multiple companies. It provides real-time insights into portfolio health, action items, activities, and communications all in one place.
overview.whoUsesIt: All team members use Mission Control as their daily hub for monitoring projects, tracking tasks, reviewing updates, and managing communications within a company context.

keyBenefits.0: Centralized dashboard for all critical information
keyBenefits.1: Real-time portfolio health and status monitoring
keyBenefits.2: Consolidated action items and tasks in one view
keyBenefits.3: Activity stream showing recent changes and updates
keyBenefits.5: Quick access to learning resources via XYREG Academy

0.title: Understanding Mission Control
0.content:
Mission Control serves as your command center for a company's operations. The dashboard is organized into several key sections:

Tabbed Interface:
- Portfolio Overview: See all critical information at a glance
- Upcoming Deadlines: Focus on time-sensitive deliverables
- Communication: Access integrated communication tools

Key Widgets:
- My Action Items: Your personal task list and assignments
- Portfolio Health: Visual status of products and projects
- Activity Stream: Recent changes and updates across the system
- XYREG Academy: Quick access to training and help resources
- Communication Hub: Messages, notifications, and collaboration

The dashboard can display data for a single company or aggregate information across all companies you have access to, depending on your selection in Client Compass.

tips.0: Use Mission Control as your daily starting point after selecting a company
tips.1: Check the Portfolio Overview tab each morning
tips.2: Keep the Activity Stream open to stay updated on changes

1.title: Using My Action Items
1.content:
The My Action Items widget shows all tasks and deliverables assigned to you:

Task Information:
- Task name and description
- Due date and priority level
- Associated product or project
- Current status and progress

Task Actions:
- Click to view full task details
- Mark tasks as complete
- Update progress and status
- Add comments and attachments
- Reassign or delegate tasks

Task Organization:
- Sorted by due date by default
- Filter by priority (high, medium, low)
- Filter by status (not started, in progress, completed)
- Filter by product or project
- Search by task name

Use the Upcoming Deadlines tab to focus specifically on time-sensitive items.

tips.0: Review your action items first thing each day
tips.1: Focus on high-priority and overdue items
tips.2: Update task status regularly to keep the team informed
tips.3: Set reminders for upcoming deadlines

2.title: Monitoring Portfolio Health
2.content:
Portfolio Health provides a visual overview of all products and projects:

Status Indicators:
- On Track (Green): Everything progressing as planned
- At Risk (Yellow): Requires attention or monitoring
- Critical (Red): Immediate action needed

Metrics Displayed:
- Number of active products
- Products by development stage
- Compliance status across portfolio
- Risk levels and open issues
- Document approval status

Interactive Features:
- Click on any metric to see detailed breakdown
- Drill down into specific products
- View trend charts and historical data
- Export reports for stakeholders

The dashboard automatically updates as data changes throughout the system, giving you always-current information.

tips.0: Review portfolio health at the start of each week
tips.1: Investigate yellow and red indicators immediately
tips.2: Use trend data to identify patterns and prevent issues
tips.3: Share portfolio health reports in team meetings

3.title: Tracking Changes with Activity Stream
3.content:
The Activity Stream shows recent updates and changes across the company:

Types of Activities:
- Document uploads and approvals
- Task assignments and completions
- Risk assessments and updates
- Compliance gap changes
- Comment and collaboration activity
- Timeline and milestone updates

Activity Details:
- Who made the change
- What was changed
- When it occurred
- Link to view full details

Filtering Options:
- Filter by activity type
- Filter by product or project
- Filter by user
- Filter by date range
- Search for specific activities

Stay informed about what is happening across all projects without having to check each module separately.

tips.0: Check the activity stream multiple times daily
tips.1: Set up filters for your areas of responsibility
tips.2: Click through to view full context of important changes
tips.3: Use activity data to prepare for team meetings

4.title: Using the Communication Hub
4.content:
Access the Communication Hub through the Communication tab in Mission Control:

Communication Features:
- Team messaging and chat
- Announcement broadcasting
- Document sharing and collaboration
- Comment threads on specific items
- Notification management

Notification Types:
- Task assignments
- Document approvals needed
- Deadline reminders
- Risk alerts
- Compliance updates
- Comment mentions

Communication Best Practices:
- Use team channels for project discussions
- Tag specific users with @ mentions
- Link to relevant documents and tasks
- Keep communication organized by topic
- Set notification preferences to avoid overload

The Communication Hub keeps all team collaboration in one place, reducing email clutter and ensuring important messages are not missed.

tips.0: Check communications at the start and end of each day
tips.1: Respond to mentions and direct messages promptly
tips.2: Use channels to organize discussions by project or topic
tips.3: Customize notifications to focus on what matters most to you

5.title: Accessing XYREG Academy
5.content:
The XYREG Academy widget provides quick access to learning resources:

Available Resources:
- Module tutorials and guides
- Video walkthroughs
- Best practice documentation
- Regulatory guidance
- Feature updates and release notes

Quick Help:
- Press F1 from anywhere to open contextual help
- Search for specific topics
- Browse by module or category
- Follow guided learning paths
- Access FAQ and troubleshooting

The Academy helps you get the most out of the platform and stay current with regulatory requirements.

tips.0: Use F1 for instant help on the current page
tips.1: Complete relevant tutorials when starting a new module
tips.2: Bookmark frequently referenced guides
tips.3: Check the Academy for new features after updates

overview.description: Product Management in XYREG enables comprehensive lifecycle management of medical devices from concept through post-market surveillance.
overview.whoUsesIt: Product Managers, Regulatory Affairs, and R&D teams use this module to track device development, classification, and regulatory submissions.

keyBenefits.1: Automated classification wizards (EU MDR, FDA, IVDR, SaMD)

0.title: Creating Your First Product
0.content:
Learn how to create and configure a new medical device product in XYREG.

Steps to Create:
1. Navigate to Company Dashboard
2. Click "Add New Product" button
3. Enter basic product information:
   - Product Name
   - Trade Name (optional)
   - Description
   - Regulatory Framework(s)
4. Select initial lifecycle phase
5. Configure product team members
6. Save and continue to classification

Required Information:
- Product Name (unique within company)
- Primary Regulatory Framework (EU MDR, FDA, ISO 13485, etc.)
- Initial Lifecycle Phase (usually Concept or Design)

Optional but Recommended:
- Trade Name for commercial products
- Detailed description for team clarity
- Upload product images for visual identification
- Assign product owner and team members

tips.0: Use clear, descriptive product names
tips.1: Add trade name if different from regulatory name
tips.2: Select all applicable regulatory frameworks upfront

1.title: Device Classification
1.content:
Use XYREG's intelligent classification wizards to determine your device's regulatory pathway.

Available Wizards:

EU MDR Classification:
- Rule-based classification (Rules 1-22)
- Medical Device vs. IVD determination
- Class I, IIa, IIb, or III assignment
- Automatic Annex requirements

FDA Classification:
- Product code lookup
- 510(k), PMA, or De Novo pathway determination
- Class I, II, or III assignment
- Predicate device identification

IVDR Classification:
- IVD-specific rules (Rules 1-7)
- Class A, B, C, or D assignment
- Performance study requirements

SaMD Classification:
- Software risk categorization
- IMDRF framework alignment
- Clinical evaluation requirements

How to Run Classification:
1. Open product page
2. Click "Classification" tab
3. Select wizard type
4. Answer guided questions
5. Review results and save
6. System auto-configures required documentation

tips.0: Run classification early in product development
tips.1: Save wizard results for audit trail
tips.2: Update classification if device changes significantly
tips.3: Use multiple wizards if selling in multiple markets

2.title: Milestone Management
2.content:
Track product development progress with intelligent milestone management.

Milestone Types:
- Phase Gates: Required deliverables for phase progression
- Regulatory Submissions: Submission deadlines and approvals
- Design Reviews: Design freeze, verification, validation
- Market Launch: CE marking, FDA clearance, commercial release
- Post-Market: PMS, vigilance, CAPA activities

Milestone Configuration:
1. Navigate to product Milestones tab
2. Add milestones manually or use templates
3. Set due dates and assign owners
4. Link related documents
5. Track completion status
6. View timeline and Gantt charts

Automatic Milestone Generation:
- Based on selected regulatory framework
- Aligned with lifecycle phases
- Customizable templates per company

Status Tracking:
🔵 Not Started
🟡 In Progress
🟢 Completed
🔴 Overdue

tips.0: Set realistic milestone dates with buffer time
tips.1: Assign clear owners for accountability
tips.2: Link required documents to milestones
tips.3: Review milestone timeline weekly

steps.0: Create product in XYREG with name "Smart Insulin Pump"
steps.1: Run EU MDR classification wizard → Result: Class IIb
steps.2: Run FDA classification wizard → Result: Class II, 510(k) required
steps.3: System generates required documentation per framework
steps.4: Set up milestones for both regulatory pathways
steps.5: Assign team members to documentation tasks

bestPractices.0: Complete classification before significant design work
bestPractices.1: Update product information as development progresses
bestPractices.2: Maintain consistent naming conventions across products
bestPractices.4: Link all relevant documents to product records
bestPractices.5: Use product variants for similar devices
bestPractices.6: Set up notifications for milestone deadlines
bestPractices.7: Archive obsolete products rather than deleting

overview.description: Document Studio is XYREG's comprehensive document management system for creating, organizing, and controlling regulatory documentation across the medical device lifecycle.
overview.whoUsesIt: All team members creating or reviewing documents - Technical Writers, QA/RA specialists, Engineers, and Managers.

keyBenefits.0: Centralized document repository with version control
keyBenefits.3: Full audit trail for regulatory compliance

0.title: Uploading Documents
0.content:
Learn how to upload and organize documents in XYREG.

Upload Methods:

Single Upload:
1. Navigate to product Documents tab
2. Click "Upload Document" button
3. Select file from computer
4. Choose document type and category
5. Add metadata (version, description, etc.)
6. Save

Bulk Upload:
1. Click "Bulk Upload" option
2. Select multiple files
3. System auto-categorizes by filename patterns
4. Review and adjust document types
5. Upload all at once

Drag & Drop:
- Drag files directly into document area
- Quick upload for multiple documents
- Auto-detection of document types

Supported Formats:
- PDF (preferred for regulatory submissions)
- Word (.docx, .doc)
- Excel (.xlsx, .xls)
- Images (.jpg, .png)
- CAD files
- Various technical formats

tips.0: Use consistent file naming conventions
tips.1: Always add meaningful descriptions
tips.2: Set correct document type for proper categorization
tips.3: Upload final versions as PDF for long-term storage

1.title: Document Version Control
1.content:
Master version control to maintain document history and ensure compliance.

Version Management:

Creating New Versions:
1. Open existing document
2. Click "Upload New Version"
3. Select updated file
4. Add version notes describing changes
5. System auto-increments version number
6. Previous versions remain accessible

Version Numbering:
- Major versions: 1.0, 2.0, 3.0 (significant changes)
- Minor versions: 1.1, 1.2, 1.3 (small updates)
- Automatic or manual numbering options

Version History:
- Complete audit trail of all versions
- Who made changes and when
- Change descriptions and rationale
- Downloadable version comparison
- Restore previous versions if needed

Document Status:
🔵 Draft: Work in progress
🟡 In Review: Under review by approvers
🟢 Approved: Official approved version
🔴 Obsolete: Superseded by newer version

tips.0: Document all changes in version notes
tips.1: Set document status appropriately
tips.2: Use version compare feature before approving
tips.3: Never delete old versions - mark as obsolete

2.title: Using Document Templates
2.content:
Accelerate document creation with templates and automated generation.

Template Types:

Company Templates:
- Reusable across all products
- Standard formats (DHF, DMR, Technical File)
- Company-specific branding and formats
- Managed by admins

Product-Specific Templates:
- Customized for specific products or device types
- Pre-filled with product data
- Regulatory framework-specific

Creating from Templates:
1. Navigate to Documents tab
2. Click "Create from Template"
3. Select appropriate template
4. System auto-fills product information
5. Complete variable sections
6. Review and save

Template Features:
- Variable placeholders auto-populated
- Consistent formatting and structure
- Required sections highlighted
- Compliance checkpoints included

tips.0: Use templates for all standard documents
tips.1: Customize templates to match company standards
tips.2: Keep template library organized by category
tips.3: Regular review and update of templates

3.title: Document Organization & Search
3.content:
Efficiently organize and find documents using XYREG's powerful features.

Organization Methods:

By Document Type:
- Design History File (DHF)
- Device Master Record (DMR)
- Technical Documentation
- Risk Management
- Clinical Evaluation
- Post-Market Surveillance

By Lifecycle Phase:
- Concept documents
- Design & Development
- Verification & Validation
- Production & Launch
- Post-Market

By Regulatory Framework:
- EU MDR documents
- FDA submission documents
- ISO certifications
- Market-specific requirements

Advanced Search:
- Full-text search across all documents
- Filter by type, phase, status, date
- Tag-based organization
- Custom metadata fields
- Saved search queries

Document Linking:
- Link related documents
- Cross-reference requirements
- Trace document dependencies
- Impact analysis for changes

tips.0: Use consistent tagging for easy retrieval
tips.1: Set up saved searches for common queries
tips.2: Link related documents for traceability
tips.3: Regular cleanup of obsolete documents

overview.description: Compliance & Gap Analysis helps you systematically assess your medical device against regulatory requirements, identify gaps, and track remediation efforts across EU MDR, FDA, ISO 13485, and other frameworks.
overview.whoUsesIt: Regulatory Affairs Managers, Quality Assurance teams, and Consultants use this module to ensure complete compliance before regulatory submissions.

keyBenefits.0: Automated compliance assessment against multiple frameworks

0.title: Running Your First Gap Analysis
0.content:
Gap Analysis systematically compares your current state against regulatory requirements to identify missing documentation, processes, or evidence.

How to Run:
1. Navigate to product Compliance tab
2. Click "Run Gap Analysis" button
3. Select regulatory framework (EU MDR, FDA 510(k), ISO 13485, etc.)
4. Choose analysis depth:
   - Quick Scan: High-level overview (5-10 min)
   - Detailed Analysis: Comprehensive assessment (30-60 min)
   - Audit Preparation: Full regulatory readiness review (2-3 hours)
5. System analyzes:
   - Required vs. existing documents
   - Process completeness
   - Evidence sufficiency
   - Regulatory timeline alignment

Analysis Outputs:
- Gap identification report
- Compliance percentage by category
- Priority recommendations
- Remediation roadmap
- Timeline estimates

tips.0: Run gap analysis early in development to plan ahead
tips.1: Re-run after major changes or document updates
tips.2: Use detailed analysis before regulatory submissions
tips.3: Export reports for stakeholder reviews

1.title: Understanding Gap Categories
1.content:
Gaps are categorized by type and severity to help you prioritize remediation efforts.

Gap Types:

Documentation Gaps:
- Missing required documents
- Incomplete document sections
- Outdated document versions
- Missing signatures or approvals

Process Gaps:
- Missing QMS procedures
- Incomplete verification/validation
- Inadequate risk management
- Missing design reviews

Evidence Gaps:
- Insufficient test data
- Missing clinical evidence
- Incomplete traceability
- Inadequate design history

Compliance Gaps:
- Missing regulatory certifications
- Expired notified body approvals
- Non-conforming labeling
- Missing post-market surveillance plans

Severity Levels:

🔴 Critical (Must fix before submission):
- Prevents regulatory approval
- Major compliance violation
- High patient safety risk

🟡 Major (Should fix soon):
- Significant compliance issue
- May delay approval
- Moderate risk

🟢 Minor (Recommend fixing):
- Best practice improvement
- Low risk
- May be questioned in audit

tips.0: Always address critical gaps first
tips.1: Create remediation plans for major gaps
tips.2: Bundle minor gaps for efficiency

2.title: Gap Remediation Workflow
2.content:
Turn identified gaps into actionable remediation tasks with progress tracking.

Remediation Process:

1. Gap Review & Assignment:
- Review all identified gaps
- Assign owners for each gap
- Set due dates based on priority
- Allocate resources and budget

2. Create Remediation Plan:
- Define specific actions required
- Identify dependencies
- Estimate effort and timeline
- Get stakeholder approval

3. Execute Remediation:
- Complete required documentation
- Implement missing processes
- Gather evidence
- Conduct verification activities
- Link completed work to gap items

4. Verification:
- Review remediation completeness
- Verify against requirements
- Document evidence
- Obtain approvals
- Mark gap as resolved

5. Re-Analysis:
- Run gap analysis again
- Confirm gap closure
- Identify any new gaps
- Update compliance metrics

tips.0: Assign realistic due dates with buffer time
tips.1: Track dependencies between gaps
tips.2: Hold regular remediation review meetings
tips.3: Celebrate milestone completions

3.title: Multi-Framework Compliance
3.content:
Manage compliance across multiple regulatory frameworks simultaneously.

Supported Frameworks:

EU MDR (Medical Device Regulation):
- Annex I-XVII requirements
- Technical documentation structure
- Clinical evaluation requirements
- Post-market surveillance obligations

FDA (Food & Drug Administration):
- 510(k) submission requirements
- PMA (Premarket Approval) documentation
- De Novo pathway requirements
- QSR (Quality System Regulation)

ISO 13485 (QMS Standard):
- Quality management system requirements
- Process documentation
- Risk management integration
- Verification and validation

ISO 14971 (Risk Management):
- Risk management process
- Risk analysis requirements
- Risk evaluation criteria
- Risk control measures

Cross-Framework Features:
- Identify common requirements across frameworks
- Reuse documentation where applicable
- Flag framework-specific gaps
- Optimize compliance strategy
- Generate framework-specific reports

tips.0: Start with primary market framework
tips.1: Look for overlap to reduce duplication
tips.2: Maintain framework-specific documentation where required
tips.3: Plan multi-market strategy early

0.scenario: Preparing for EU MDR Technical Documentation Submission
0.description: Complete gap analysis workflow for a Class IIb medical device

steps.0: Run detailed gap analysis for EU MDR Annex II & III
steps.1: System identifies 47 gaps: 12 critical, 23 major, 12 minor
steps.2: Review critical gaps: missing clinical evaluation, incomplete risk management
steps.3: Assign gaps to team: Clinical to Regulatory, Risk to Quality Engineer
steps.5: Execute: Complete clinical literature review, update risk analysis
steps.6: Upload completed documents and link to gap items
steps.8: Re-run gap analysis: Now 0 critical, 8 major remaining
steps.10: Continue with major gap remediation before submission

0.expectedOutcome: Complete Technical Documentation ready for Notified Body submission

1.description: Simultaneous FDA 510(k) and EU MDR preparation

steps.0: Run gap analysis for both FDA 510(k) and EU MDR
steps.1: Identify common gaps: Design verification, risk management, labeling
steps.2: Create unified remediation plan addressing both frameworks
steps.4: Track progress against both frameworks in parallel
steps.5: Generate separate compliance reports for FDA and EU

1.expectedOutcome: Optimized documentation strategy serving both markets efficiently

bestPractices.0: Run gap analysis at multiple project phases, not just at the end
bestPractices.1: Use detailed analysis mode before regulatory submissions
bestPractices.2: Assign clear ownership for each identified gap
bestPractices.3: Set realistic remediation timelines with contingency buffers
bestPractices.5: Link all completed work back to specific gaps for traceability
bestPractices.6: Re-run analysis after major product or process changes
bestPractices.7: Maintain evidence for gap closure (documents, test results, approvals)
bestPractices.8: Use gap analysis reports in design reviews and management meetings
bestPractices.9: Export compliance reports for stakeholders and auditors
bestPractices.10: Address critical gaps immediately - they block submissions
bestPractices.11: Bundle related gaps for efficient remediation

overview.description: Design & Risk Management provides comprehensive tools for managing design controls per ISO 13485 and risk management per ISO 14971, ensuring your medical device meets safety and performance requirements.
overview.whoUsesIt: Design Engineers, Risk Managers, Quality Assurance, and Regulatory Affairs teams working on medical device development.

0.title: Creating Risk Assessments
0.content:
Systematic risk identification and analysis following ISO 14971 principles.

Risk Management Process:

1. Risk Analysis Planning:
- Define scope and boundaries
- Identify intended use and reasonably foreseeable misuse
- Determine characteristics related to safety
- Set up risk acceptance criteria
- Assign risk management team

2. Hazard Identification:
- Use structured brainstorming
- Review similar device hazards
- Analyze user interface risks
- Consider manufacturing risks
- Include software hazards (for SaMD)

Common Hazard Categories:
- Energy hazards (electrical, thermal, mechanical)
- Biological hazards (biocompatibility, infection)
- Environmental hazards (EMC, radiation)
- Operational hazards (use error, training)
- Information hazards (incorrect output, data security)

3. Risk Estimation:
- Assess severity of harm
- Estimate probability of occurrence
- Calculate risk level (Severity × Probability)
- Use risk matrix or scoring system

Severity Levels:
- Catastrophic: Death or permanent injury
- Critical: Serious injury requiring intervention
- Major: Injury requiring medical attention
- Minor: Temporary discomfort, no medical intervention
- Negligible: No injury

Probability Levels:
- Frequent: Expected to occur repeatedly
- Probable: Likely to occur several times
- Occasional: May occur sometime
- Remote: Unlikely but possible
- Improbable: Extremely unlikely

tips.0: Involve multi-disciplinary team in hazard identification
tips.1: Use FMEA templates for structured analysis
tips.2: Consider entire product lifecycle, not just use
tips.3: Document assumptions and rationale clearly

1.title: Implementing Risk Controls
1.content:
Apply risk control measures following ISO 14971 hierarchy to reduce risks to acceptable levels.

Risk Control Hierarchy (in order of preference):

1. Inherently Safe Design:
- Eliminate hazard through design
- Most effective control method
- Examples: Remove sharp edges, use low voltage, eliminate toxic materials

2. Protective Measures in Device/Manufacturing:
- Add safety features when elimination not possible
- Examples: Alarms, interlocks, redundant systems, error-proofing

3. Information for Safety:
- Warnings, precautions, training when technical controls insufficient
- Examples: User manual warnings, labeling, training requirements

Implementing Controls:
1. Select appropriate control measure(s)
2. Implement in design or process
3. Verify effectiveness
4. Re-assess residual risk
5. Document implementation

Verification Methods:
- Design analysis
- Testing (bench, animal, clinical)
- Inspection
- User studies (for usability risks)
- Simulation
- Literature review

Residual Risk:
- Risk remaining after control implementation
- Must be acceptable per criteria
- Document benefit-risk analysis if elevated

tips.0: Always try inherent safety first before adding warnings
tips.1: Combine multiple control types for high risks
tips.2: Verify control effectiveness with objective evidence
tips.3: Update risk controls when design changes

2.title: Design Review Process
2.content:
Formal design reviews ensure comprehensive evaluation at key development stages per 21 CFR 820.30.

Design Review Types:

1. Concept Review:
- Timing: After initial concept definition
- Objectives: Evaluate feasibility, review user needs, assess regulatory pathway
- Deliverables: Concept approval, project go/no-go

2. Design Input Review:
- Timing: After requirements finalization
- Objectives: Complete requirements, traceability, verification planning
- Deliverables: Approved design inputs

3. Design Output Review:
- Timing: After design specification completion
- Objectives: Design outputs meet inputs, manufacturing feasibility
- Deliverables: Approved design specifications

4. Design Transfer Review:
- Timing: Before full-scale production
- Objectives: Production readiness, process validation complete
- Deliverables: Design transfer approval

5. Final Design Review:
- Timing: Before first commercial distribution
- Objectives: All design controls complete, regulatory submission ready
- Deliverables: Design freeze, commercial release approval

Review Components:
- Documented agenda and objectives
- Multi-disciplinary team participation
- Independent reviewers
- Action item tracking
- Formal approval signatures

tips.0: Schedule reviews in advance at project milestones
tips.1: Prepare review package 1 week ahead
tips.2: Assign action items with owners and due dates
tips.3: Track open items before next review

3.title: Design Traceability Matrix
3.content:
Maintain complete traceability from user needs through design, verification, and risk controls.

Traceability Links:

User Needs → Design Inputs:
- Map stakeholder requirements to technical requirements
- Ensure all user needs addressed
- Regulatory requirements included

Design Inputs → Design Outputs:
- Link requirements to specifications
- Ensure complete implementation
- Verify nothing missing

Design Outputs → Verification:
- Each specification has test method
- Link test results to outputs
- Demonstrate conformance

Design Inputs → Validation:
- Validate that user needs met
- Clinical evaluation evidence
- Usability validation

Risks → Controls → Verification:
- Each risk has control measure
- Controls are verified effective
- Residual risk acceptable

Matrix Management in XYREG:
1. System auto-generates initial matrix
2. Manually link related elements
3. Identify gaps (missing links)
4. Export for regulatory submissions
5. Update as design evolves
6. Use for impact analysis

tips.0: Maintain matrix throughout development, not at end
tips.1: Review matrix completeness in design reviews
tips.2: Use matrix for change impact analysis
tips.3: Export matrix for technical documentation

bestPractices.0: Conduct risk analysis early and update continuously
bestPractices.1: Use structured methods (FMEA, FTA) for consistency
bestPractices.2: Involve multi-disciplinary teams in risk identification
bestPractices.3: Follow ISO 14971 risk control hierarchy strictly
bestPractices.4: Verify all risk controls with objective evidence
bestPractices.5: Schedule design reviews at key project milestones
bestPractices.6: Maintain complete traceability matrix throughout development

overview.description: Business Analysis tools help you evaluate the financial viability of medical device projects through NPV calculations, market analysis, and regulatory cost modeling.
overview.whoUsesIt: Business Development, Product Managers, Finance teams, and Executives making go/no-go decisions on medical device projects.

keyBenefits.0: NPV and IRR calculations for project evaluation

0.title: Understanding NPV Analysis
0.content:
Net Present Value (NPV) analysis helps determine if a medical device project is financially viable.

NPV Formula:
NPV = Sum of (Cash Flow / (1 + Discount Rate)^Year) - Initial Investment

Key Components:

Revenue Streams:
- Unit sales projections by market
- Pricing strategy
- Market penetration assumptions
- Reimbursement considerations
- Service and maintenance revenue

Cost Categories:
- Development costs (R&D, design, testing)
- Regulatory costs (submissions, fees, consultants)
- Clinical trial expenses
- Manufacturing setup and COGS
- Marketing and sales
- Post-market surveillance

Time Value Considerations:
- Discount rate (typically 8-15% for med devices)
- Project timeline (typically 5-10 years)
- Cash flow timing
- Regulatory approval delays

Decision Criteria:
- NPV > 0: Project adds value, proceed
- NPV < 0: Project destroys value, reconsider
- NPV = 0: Breakeven, marginal project

Internal Rate of Return (IRR):
- Discount rate where NPV = 0
- Higher IRR = more attractive project
- Compare to company's hurdle rate
- Typical med device IRR: 15-30%

tips.0: Use conservative revenue estimates to avoid over-optimism
tips.1: Include all hidden costs (regulatory consultants, clinical trials)
tips.2: Model multiple scenarios (best/likely/worst case)
tips.3: Update NPV regularly as project progresses

1.title: Smart Cost Intelligence System
1.content:
AI-powered cost estimation for accurate regulatory cost modeling by market.

How It Works:

1. Market Selection:
- Choose target markets (US, EU, Japan, etc.)
- System loads market-specific cost templates
- Real-time currency conversion
- Regulatory framework requirements

2. Device Complexity Analysis:
- Classification determines cost multipliers
- Class I: 1.0x baseline
- Class II: 1.2-1.5x baseline
- Class III: 1.8-2.5x baseline

3. Cost Categories:

Regulatory Submission:
- Application fees (fixed per market)
- Consultant fees (if needed)
- Translation costs
- Technical file preparation

Clinical Evidence:
- Literature review
- Clinical investigation design
- Study conduct costs
- CRO fees

Certification & Compliance:
- Notified Body fees (EU)
- Annual surveillance fees
- QMS certification
- Testing and validation

4. Scenario Planning:
- Conservative: +15-25% buffer
- Typical: Market standard costs
- Aggressive: -10-20% optimized

tips.0: Start with typical scenario, then model conservative
tips.1: Use market-specific data rather than converted costs
tips.2: Factor in multi-year inflation for long timelines
tips.3: Validate estimates with actual market data when possible

2.title: Multi-Scenario Planning
2.content:
Model different business scenarios to understand risks and opportunities.

Scenario Types:

Base Case (Typical):
- Most likely outcome
- Standard market assumptions
- Normal regulatory timeline
- Expected competitive landscape

Optimistic Case (Aggressive):
- Best possible outcome
- Faster regulatory approval
- Higher market penetration
- Lower competitive pressure

Pessimistic Case (Conservative):
- Challenging outcome
- Regulatory delays
- Lower adoption rates
- Higher competition

Sensitivity Analysis:
Test impact of changing key variables:
- ±20% in unit sales
- ±6 months in regulatory timeline
- ±15% in pricing
- ±25% in development costs
- ±2% in discount rate

Break-Even Analysis:
- Units to sell to recover investment
- Time to profitability
- Critical success factors
- Decision trees for go/no-go

Portfolio Analysis:
- Compare multiple projects
- Resource allocation decisions
- Risk-return optimization
- Strategic prioritization

tips.0: Always model at least 3 scenarios
tips.1: Weight scenarios by probability for expected value
tips.2: Update scenarios as market data emerges
tips.3: Use sensitivity analysis to identify critical assumptions

overview.description: Clinical Trials Management helps you plan, execute, and document clinical investigations for medical devices across different regulatory frameworks.
overview.whoUsesIt: Clinical Affairs, Regulatory Affairs, and R&D teams conducting clinical studies to demonstrate safety and performance.

keyBenefits.0: Clinical study planning and protocol management

0.title: Understanding Study Types
0.content:
Different clinical study types serve different regulatory and evidence needs.

Study Types:

Feasibility Studies:
- Purpose: Initial safety and performance assessment
- Size: 5-20 patients typically
- Duration: 3-6 months
- Use: Proof of concept, design refinement

Pivotal Studies:
- Purpose: Demonstrate safety and effectiveness for approval
- Size: 50-300+ patients
- Duration: 6-36 months
- Use: Primary evidence for regulatory submission

Post-Market Surveillance (PMS):
- Purpose: Long-term safety and performance monitoring
- Size: Ongoing patient population
- Duration: Continuous (5+ years typical)
- Use: Real-world evidence, ongoing compliance

Registries:
- Purpose: Collect long-term outcomes data
- Size: Large patient populations
- Duration: Multi-year or indefinite
- Use: Real-world evidence, comparative effectiveness

tips.0: Start with feasibility study for novel devices
tips.1: Size pivotal study based on statistical power calculation
tips.2: Plan PMS early to meet EU MDR requirements
tips.3: Consider registry participation for long-term data

1.title: Developing Clinical Protocols
1.content:
Well-designed protocols are essential for successful clinical investigations.

Protocol Components:

1. Study Overview:
- Study title and identifier
- Study type and phase
- Indication and patient population
- Study objectives (primary and secondary)
- Study design (randomized, blinded, controlled)

2. Study Endpoints:

Primary Endpoint:
- Main measure of device effectiveness
- Must be clinically meaningful
- Powers sample size calculation

Secondary Endpoints:
- Support primary endpoint
- Safety measures
- Quality of life assessments

3. Patient Selection:
- Inclusion criteria: Target patient demographics
- Exclusion criteria: Safety concerns, confounding conditions

4. Study Procedures:
- Screening and enrollment
- Device use procedures
- Follow-up schedule
- Data collection methods
- Adverse event reporting

5. Statistical Analysis Plan:
- Sample size justification
- Primary analysis method
- Handling of missing data
- Success criteria

tips.0: Engage statistician early for sample size calculation
tips.1: Use validated outcome measures when possible
tips.2: Plan realistic follow-up schedule
tips.3: Include clear adverse event definitions

2.title: Managing Sites and Investigators
2.content:
Effective site and investigator management ensures study quality and timeline adherence.

Site Selection:

Evaluation Criteria:
- Patient population access
- PI qualifications and experience
- Site infrastructure and capabilities
- Track record in clinical research
- Regulatory compliance history

Site Initiation:
1. Site qualification visit
2. Contract and budget negotiation
3. Ethics committee submission
4. Protocol training
5. Site initiation visit (SIV)
6. First patient enrollment

Ongoing Management:

Site Monitoring:
- Regular monitoring visits
- Data quality checks
- Protocol compliance verification
- AE/SAE review

Performance Tracking:
- Enrollment rate by site
- Data quality scores
- Protocol deviations
- Patient retention
- Timeline adherence

tips.0: Over-recruit sites by 20-30% for enrollment targets
tips.1: Provide comprehensive site training upfront
tips.2: Monitor actively, especially early in study
tips.3: Maintain good investigator relationships

3.title: Working with CRO Partners
3.content:
Contract Research Organizations (CROs) can accelerate trials and provide expertise.

CRO Services:
- Full-service: Complete trial management
- Functional: Specific services (monitoring, data management)
- Site management organizations (SMOs)
- Central labs and imaging core labs
- Biostatistics and data analysis

CRO Selection:
- Therapeutic area expertise
- Geographic coverage
- Technology platforms
- Quality track record
- Cost and timeline
- Regulatory experience

CRO Management in XYREG:
- CRO partner registry
- Service agreement tracking
- Performance monitoring
- Budget and payment tracking
- Deliverable management

Working Effectively:
- Clear SOW (Statement of Work)
- Defined roles and responsibilities
- Regular status meetings
- Transparent communication
- Quality metrics tracking
- Change order management

tips.0: Get multiple CRO bids for comparison
tips.1: Check CRO references thoroughly
tips.2: Clearly define deliverables and timelines
tips.3: Monitor CRO performance actively

overview.description: Comprehensive guide to startup funding stages with MedTech-specific context, investor expectations, and regulatory milestone alignment.
overview.whoUsesIt: Founders, executives, and business development professionals seeking investment for medical device ventures.

keyBenefits.2: Know typical amounts and dilution at each stage

0.title: Understanding Funding Stages
0.content: Medical device startups progress through funding stages aligned with regulatory milestones. Unlike software, MedTech has regulatory gates, clinical evidence requirements, and longer timelines (3-7 years). Key stages: Pre-Seed (€50K-500K) for proof of concept, Seed (€500K-3M) for design freeze, Series A (€3M-15M) for clinical trials, Series B (€15M-50M) for commercial launch. Non-dilutive options include grants (Horizon Europe, SBIR), venture debt, and strategic partnerships.

tips.0: Align funding with regulatory milestones
tips.1: Consider non-dilutive before dilutive
tips.2: Higher device class = more capital needed

1.title: Pre-Seed & Seed Funding
1.content: Pre-Seed (€50K-500K): Earliest capital for technical feasibility, IP protection, team building, and market validation. Investors: Friends & family, angels, accelerators. Seed (€500K-3M): First institutional round for design development, bench testing, regulatory strategy, and team expansion. Investors: Seed VCs, strategic angels, corporate venture arms. At seed, investors want: proof of concept, regulatory pathway, IP strategy, clinical advisors. Class-specific needs: Class I (€500K-1M), Class II 510k (€1-2M), Class III (€2-3M).

tips.0: Pre-seed proves viability with minimal capital
tips.1: Clinical advisors are critical at seed
tips.2: Higher class = larger seed round

2.title: Series A & Series B
2.content: Series A (€3M-15M): Major growth round funding clinical trials, regulatory submissions, manufacturing scale-up, and commercial preparation. Investors evaluate clinical data, regulatory de-risking, commercial opportunity, team, and IP moat. Series B (€15M-50M): Expansion after regulatory clearance for commercial launch, sales team, international expansion, and reimbursement. By Series B, investors want regulatory approval, commercial proof points, unit economics, and clear exit path.

tips.0: Series A funds trials and submission
tips.1: Series B requires commercial traction
tips.2: Strategic investors often enter as M&A precursor

3.title: Bridge & Alternative Funding
3.content: Bridge Rounds (€500K-5M): Short-term financing between rounds via convertible notes or SAFEs. Use when awaiting regulatory decision or clinical readout. Non-Dilutive Options: Government grants (Horizon Europe €2-5M, EIC up to €2.5M grant, NIH SBIR), venture debt (after Series A, 10-15% interest), and strategic partnerships (R&D, distribution, licensing). Benefits: no dilution, investor validation. Challenges: long timelines, reporting requirements.

tips.0: Bridge needs clear milestone purpose
tips.1: Grants extend runway without dilution
tips.2: Venture debt works post-Series A

4.title: What Investors Expect
4.content: Pre-Seed: Compelling team, unmet clinical need, proof of concept, initial IP. Seed: Working prototype, design freeze, regulatory pathway, clinical advisors. Series A: Complete preclinical package, pre-submission feedback, clinical trial design, manufacturing partner. Series B: Regulatory approval, commercial sales, reimbursement wins, scalable model. Red flags at any stage: unclear regulatory pathway, unprotected IP, team conflicts, unrealistic timelines.

tips.0: Expectations escalate each round
tips.1: Regulatory de-risking is key
tips.2: Team gaps must be acknowledged

5.title: Determining Your Funding Need
5.content: Golden rule: Raise enough to reach a value-inflecting milestone. Steps: (1) Identify next major milestone, (2) Build bottom-up budget (R&D 30-40%, Clinical/Reg 20-30%, Manufacturing 10-20%, G&A 15-25%), (3) Add 30-50% buffer for delays, (4) Validate against benchmarks. Most VCs want 18-24 months runway. Class I: Pre-Seed €100-300K, Seed €500K-1M. Class II: Pre-Seed €200-400K, Seed €1-2M, Series A €5-10M. Class III: Pre-Seed €300-500K, Seed €2-3M, Series A €10-20M, Series B €30-50M.

tips.0: Raise to value-inflecting milestone
tips.1: 18-24 months is standard runway
tips.2: Add 30-50% buffer for MedTech delays

Explains the Company vs. Device distinction

Tips and common mistakes from real-world medtech experience

0.title: Section 0: Client Compass — Multi-Company Management

tips.0: Use status indicators to prioritise which company needs attention first
tips.1: Set up company profiles completely — incomplete profiles create issues in regulatory documents
tips.2: If you manage multiple companies, use Client Compass as your starting point each day
